preamble: The transformation of the voiced alveolar fricative [z] into an alveolar rhotic [r] is known as rhotacism, a classic historical sound change documented in several Indo-European and Austronesian languages.

examples.note: Intervocalic [s] voiced to [z] and then regularly shifted to [r] in the development of Classical Latin.
Historical shift in West and North Germanic where the voiced sibilant from Verner's Law became a rhotic (e.g., English 'was' vs. 'were').
